personas.name: Rashid — Bangladeshi Construction Worker
personas.icp: Bangladeshi work-permit holder on a construction site, S$700–900/mo basic wage, lives in a purpose-built dormitory (e.g. Tuas View, Kranji Lodge, Westlite Toh Guan), remits ~60% of earnings home, gets one rest day per week, buys staples from on-site canteen / Geylang Serai mama shops.
pains: Rice/oil/instant-noodle prices at on-site canteens are 30–50% above NTUC retail with no choice
Only 1 rest day per week (often Sunday) — no time for a full FairPrice run before it closes
Carrying a 5kg rice bag back on the bus from Mustafa is physically painful after a 12-hour shift
PayNow yes, but bank transfers home to BD eat 2–3% so every spare dollar matters
Bulk-buy with roommates is the survival strategy; no one wants to be the guy who overpays
personas.current_workaround: On-site canteen vendor (markup 30–50%) for emergencies + a weekend bus to Mustafa Centre or Tekka Market once a month for the bulk haul + WhatsApp group orders organised by a roommate-de-facto-leader who collects cash.
personas.nba: On-site canteen: 5kg jasmine rice S$18 (vs NTUC S$11–13), 1L cooking oil S$5.50 (vs NTUC S$3.20), Maggi 5-pack S$3.50 (vs NTUC S$2.40). Status-quo cost for a basic weekly basket = S$40–45 vs NTUC-equivalent S$28. Monthly canteen-tax penalty: ~S$50/mo on a S$200 grocery spend (25% overpayment vs heartland retail). Mustafa run alternative: 4hr round-trip × bus fare S$4.80 + opportunity cost of his only rest day (priceless to him).

personas.name: Maria — Filipino Domestic Helper / Service Worker
personas.icp: Filipino domestic helper (live-in) or F&B/retail service worker (live-out, S-pass-adjacent), S$600–800/mo, off-day Sunday shopper at Lucky Plaza / Peninsula Plaza / Bugis, sends ~50% home via Western Union / Wise, buys vitamins and personal care for self + ships balikbayan boxes home.
pains: Centrum/Caltrate/Berocca at Watsons/Guardian retails at S$45–55 — half her weekly allowance gone in one bottle
Lucky Plaza freight forwarders mark up vitamins inside balikbayan kits by 20–30% over Watsons retail
She's been told by family back home to send Centrum specifically (brand trust = non-negotiable; no generic substitution)
Sunday is her ONE off-day; standing in queue at Watsons Lucky Plaza costs her social time
PayNow yes; but most Lucky Plaza shops still prefer cash, so she carries S$200+ on Sunday
personas.current_workaround: Watsons / Guardian at Lucky Plaza on Sunday off-day (full retail); occasional Shopee 11.11/Lazada 12.12 hauls when her employer's wifi is available; balikbayan freight-forwarder vitamin kits (marked up 20–30% but bundled with shipping convenience).
personas.nba: Watsons retail Centrum Adults 60s = S$48.90 (April 2026), Caltrate Plus 60s = S$42.90. Lucky Plaza freight-forwarder kits typically S$60 for the same combo + ship-home fee. Shopee 11.11 best price seen ~S$32 for Centrum 60s but only on flash hours, plus 5–7 day waits. Status-quo monthly health-spend: S$45–60 every 6–8 weeks = ~S$8–10/mo amortised, with 1–2 hours of Sunday queue time (opportunity cost of her one rest day).

personas.name: Wei Lin — Mainland Chinese / Malaysian S-Pass Holder
personas.icp: Mainland Chinese or Malaysian S-pass holder in tech/finance/healthcare ops, S$3,000–6,000/mo, rents an HDB room or shares a condo, Telegram-and-Xiaohongshu-fluent, deal-hunts via Shopback / Fave / SG Deals Telegram channels, will go out of her way for a 50%-off Centrum because she sees it as 'smart shopping' not 'poverty shopping'.
pains: Watsons retail feels like 'paying tourist tax'; she knows there's a cheaper way and resents missing it
Mustafa is far from her CBD/Bishan/Tampines orbit; the deal must come to her, not vice versa
Time-poor: 10-hour days, no time for grocery arbitrage, but she'll spend 15 minutes on a Telegram flash-sale alert
She's the de facto group-buy organiser for her WhatsApp friend cluster — needs the deal to be shareable / referrable
Trust signal matters: 'near-expiry' must be transparently labelled, not hidden — she'll forgive a 6-month expiry but not a 'huh, this is expiring next week' surprise
personas.current_workaround: Shopback cashback on Watsons online (1–4% back) + Shopee/Lazada flash sales for vitamins + Tao Bao haul once a quarter for non-perishables + occasional iHerb international order (4-week wait, hit-or-miss customs).
personas.nba: Watsons online with Shopback Centrum 60s = S$48.90 − ~S$1.50 cashback ≈ S$47.40. iHerb Centrum 200ct from US$32 + freight ≈ S$55 landed. Shopee 11.11 / 12.12 flash window: S$30–35 for Centrum 60s. Status-quo monthly health-and-deal-hunt budget: S$80–150/mo. Time cost: ~3 hr/mo scrolling deal channels — she pays in attention, not dollars.

personas.name: Auntie Sim — Singaporean HDB Heartland Value Shopper
personas.icp: Singaporean PR/citizen, 45–65, lives in a HDB heartland (Yishun, Tampines, Woodlands, Jurong), Sheng Siong / NTUC FairPrice loyalty card holder, NTUC Plus member, will travel one MRT stop for a S$2 saving on rice, telegrams photos of price tags to her sisters' WhatsApp group, retired or part-time work.
pains: Inflation 2024–2026 has eaten her grocery budget; same basket costs 12–18% more than 2 years ago
Sheng Siong house brand is good but limited; some items (vitamins, branded snacks) still need NTUC or Watsons
She doesn't trust 'near-expiry' labels by default — burned once by a Don Don Donki bento (paranoia anchor)
PayNow she uses; but her real anchor is the NTUC Plus link card and Sheng Siong Saturday flyer
Won't buy in bulk if pantry storage is tight; HDB kitchen is small, no garage / cellar
personas.current_workaround: Sheng Siong weekly flyer + NTUC FairPrice Plus discount runs + Giant clearance shelf scavenging + Don Don Donki end-of-day bento for dinner (when prices drop after 7pm) + occasional Shopee deals for non-perishables.
personas.nba: NTUC FairPrice 5kg jasmine rice S$11.95 / Sheng Siong S$10.50 / Giant clearance S$9.20. Centrum 60s at NTUC Unity S$45.90 with NTUC Plus 4% rebate ≈ S$44.10 effective. Don Don Donki 50%-off bento at 8pm ≈ S$4 dinner. Status-quo monthly grocery + health spend: S$300–450 with 5–10% saved via flyer arbitrage = ~S$25–40/mo of self-organised discount hunt. Time cost: 1–2 supermarket trips per week, ~4 hr/wk for shopping.

personas.name: HR Sandra — Welfare / Dorm-Operator Bulk Buyer
personas.icp: HR/admin manager or dorm-welfare officer at a construction/marine/landscaping company (50–500 foreign workers) or a PBD operator (e.g. Centurion, ASPRI-Westlite, Ascent), buys bulk welfare supplies (Hari Raya / CNY hampers, monthly rice + oil welfare packs, vitamin packs for wellness day), pays via company PayNow / corporate credit card / 30-day invoice.
pains: MOM / dormitory licensing audits push her to demonstrate worker welfare spend — she needs an invoice, not a kopitiam receipt
Bulk welfare hampers from Sheng Siong / FairPrice Xtra are functional but lack a 'we care' brand wrapper
She's quoted by 3 different traders for a CNY hamper run; opaque pricing — 'why is your rice S$2 more than the next guy?'
Delivery to multi-site dorms (Tuas + Kranji + Mandai on the same day) is logistically painful; most vendors won't split-deliver
She wants a self-serve B2B portal but ends up on WhatsApp with a Chinese trader's PA
personas.current_workaround: Direct calls/WhatsApp to 2–3 commodity traders (Tan Sang Hee, Sin Heng Chan, Tong Garden distributors) for bulk rice/oil; FairPrice Xtra Business for hamper runs; ad-hoc Lazada/Shopee bulk for off-festival months; Mustafa wholesale for one-off welfare events.
personas.nba: Bulk rice (50× 5kg bags) at trader: S$10.50/bag = S$525 + GST + free delivery to one site only. FairPrice Xtra Business hamper: S$35/hamper × 100 = S$3,500. Mustafa wholesale: ~10–15% below FairPrice for staples but no e-invoice and no multi-site delivery. Status-quo monthly welfare-spend cycle: S$2,000–8,000/mo with 4–8 hours of Sandra's procurement time at a fully-loaded SG HR rate of S$35/hr ≈ S$140–280/mo opportunity cost on procurement admin alone.

pricing_models.name: Per-SKU clearance % off retail
pricing_models.rationale: Discounter's natural model — surplus / near-expiry FMCG sourced batch-by-batch, each SKU listed with a transparent S$ price and a 'vs retail' anchor (NTUC / Watsons RRP). Headline 'up to 80% off' lives at the page level; per-SKU shows the real number. Easy to audit, easy to scale.

pricing_models.name: Bundle pricing (S$5 / S$15 / S$40 baskets)
pricing_models.rationale: Pre-curated bundles solve choice paralysis for time-poor dorm workers and unlock the S$40 free-delivery threshold. The S$5 essentials bundle is a viral acquisition wedge; the S$40 weekly bundle is the LTV vehicle; the S$15 vitamin combo is the FlashCart anchor.

pricing_models.name: Group-buy / bulk B2B invoice
pricing_models.rationale: For employer/dorm-operator welfare buys: NET-30 invoice, multi-site delivery quoted as a line-item, transparent unit pricing visible to the HR auditor. Dovetails with WSG worker-welfare narratives and MOM dorm-operator licensing requirements.

pricing_models.name: Loyalty / referral card (cross-product)
pricing_models.rationale: Stamp card / referral credit that unlocks across Discounter (online dorm delivery) and FlashCart (in-person flash sales). 'Refer 3 dorm-mates, your next S$40 bundle is S$30.' Builds the WhatsApp word-of-mouth loop that this ICP runs on; cheaper CAC than paid ads to a non-English-search audience.

pricing_models.name: Flat-rate flash-sale (FlashCart event pricing)
pricing_models.rationale: Single fixed price per event — 'Centrum + Caltrate combo S$25 today only, dorm courtyard 6–9pm.' No app, no scrolling, no choice fatigue. Pure point-of-sale PayNow conversion. Tradeoff: caps unit margin per visit, but the trust + footfall gain at a single dorm event compounds into the Discounter app install funnel.

elasticity_heuristics.evidence: FMCG staples for sub-S$1k/mo earners are textbook high-elasticity. Field-research signal: TWC2 and HOME case-worker reports (2024-2025) repeatedly cite dormitory-canteen rice/oil markups as the #1 grievance after wage disputes. A S$2 difference on a 5kg rice bag (15% delta) reliably swings buying behaviour at Geylang Serai mama shops — observable on weekend foot-traffic patterns. Group-buy WhatsApp organisers in BD-worker dorms quote a ~S$5 'this is worth assembling 8 guys for' threshold; below that they don't bother.

elasticity_heuristics.segment: Maria — Filipino Domestic Helper / Service Worker
elasticity_heuristics.evidence: Mixed picture: highly elastic on substitutable items (snacks, cooking essentials sent in balikbayan boxes) but inelastic on trusted-brand vitamins. Centrum specifically commands brand loyalty bordering on cargo-cult — Watsons Lucky Plaza staff anecdotally report 'they ask for Centrum, they refuse the generic equivalent even at 40% off.' Pfizer's Centrum SG retail has held S$45–50 price band through 2023–2026 inflation cycle without observed volume collapse. Caltrate similar. So: elastic on basket size, inelastic on which brand fills the bottle slot.

elasticity_heuristics.segment: Wei Lin — Mainland Chinese / Malaysian S-Pass Holder
elasticity_heuristics.evidence: She's price-sensitive but in the 'smart-shopper' framing, not 'survival' framing. Shopback partner data and Shopee SG flash-sale conversion patterns (publicly cited in their 11.11 2025 press release: 4.2× conversion lift at >40% discount thresholds) suggest she'll act when discount crosses ~40% off retail. Below 30% off, the deal-hunt instinct doesn't trigger. She also cares about expiry transparency in a way Rashid doesn't — perceived risk amplifies elasticity at the trust margin.

elasticity_heuristics.segment: Auntie Sim — Singaporean HDB Heartland Value Shopper
elasticity_heuristics.evidence: SG heartland shopper behaviour is well-documented: NTUC's own financial reports cite Plus-card promotional uplift of 8–15% on flagged SKUs. Sheng Siong's house-brand penetration grew from 8% to 14% of basket between 2020–2025 as inflation bit. But she has an absolute floor on perceived quality — she'll switch from Jasmine premium to Songhe house brand to save S$2, but won't touch a no-name brand even at 60% off. Trust ceiling caps elasticity on the upside.

elasticity_heuristics.segment: HR Sandra — Welfare / Dorm-Operator Bulk Buyer
elasticity_heuristics.evidence: B2B procurement with audit and licensing constraints (MOM dormitory operator standards, MOM EFW welfare provisions) is structurally low-elasticity on price; she's optimising for compliance + reliability + invoiceability, not the cheapest unit price. Switching cost from her current trader relationship is real (3-year history, NET-30 trust). She'll pay 5–10% more for split-site delivery + e-invoice compliance than for the same goods at FairPrice Xtra Business with no delivery.

recommended_tiers.name: FlashCart Dorm Combo — Centrum + Caltrate
recommended_tiers.target_persona: Maria — Filipino Domestic Helper / Service Worker
what_in: Centrum Adults 60s (Pfizer, SG-imported, ≥6-month expiry)
Caltrate Plus 60s (Pfizer, SG-imported, ≥6-month expiry)
PayNow QR at point-of-sale (no app required)
Held at dormitory courtyard or community hall flash event (3-hour window)
Receipt with ENGLISH + TAGALOG + BENGALI line items
what_excluded: Home delivery (use Discounter Weekly Bundle instead)
Bulk pack sizes (use Employer Welfare Pack instead)
Replenishment scheduling (use Discounter loyalty card instead)
recommended_tiers.psychological_anchor: Half the Watsons price for both bottles. The combo retails at S$91.80 at Watsons; you pay S$25 — that's the freight-forwarder kit cost minus the freight-forwarder.

recommended_tiers.name: Discounter S$5 Essentials Bundle
what_in: 1kg jasmine rice (clearance / surplus stock, ≥3-month expiry)
5-pack instant noodles (Indomie / Maggi / Koka rotation by batch)
500ml cooking oil (palm or canola, brand-rotating)
Self-pickup at dorm courtyard locker OR group-buy aggregator at no extra cost
PayNow at checkout, Bengali + English UX
what_excluded: Free delivery (only unlocked at S$40+ basket — see Weekly Bundle)
Choice of brand (clearance batches, rotates weekly)
Returns / exchange (final sale on near-expiry stock)
recommended_tiers.psychological_anchor: Half of one canteen meal pays for a weekly staples top-up. Canteen 1L oil is S$5.50 — at Discounter the same S$5 buys oil + rice + noodles.

recommended_tiers.name: Discounter Weekly Dormitory Bundle
what_in: 5kg jasmine rice (clearance batch)
1L cooking oil
10-pack instant noodles
1kg sugar OR salt (rotating)
2× shower gel / soap (clearance personal care)
Snack pack (biscuits / Khong Guan tin / Maggi cup)
Free delivery to dormitory address (S$40 threshold)
Bengali + Tamil + English receipt + WhatsApp order confirmation
what_excluded: Cold-chain items (no fresh meat / dairy in v1)
Multi-site / bulk allocation for whole dorm block (use Welfare Pack)
recommended_tiers.psychological_anchor: S$40 weekly = S$160/mo. Equivalent NTUC FairPrice basket S$58–65 with no delivery to dorm. You pay S$40, save S$20+, and lose zero rest-day hours. One dorm meal saved per week pays for the upgrade.

recommended_tiers.name: Heartland Value Pack (Discounter Locals)
recommended_tiers.target_persona: Auntie Sim — Singaporean HDB Heartland Value Shopper
what_in: Curated 'today's clearance' bundle (5–7 SKUs, rotating weekly)
Headline 'vs NTUC RRP' and 'vs Sheng Siong RRP' shown per item — radical price transparency
Expiry date printed clearly on listing AND box label (no hidden 'best before')
PayNow + NETS at checkout
HDB-block locker pickup OR S$3 last-mile delivery within 5km
recommended_tiers.psychological_anchor: Sheng Siong on a Tuesday flyer day — but the flyer comes to your phone, and the rice is half price because it expires in 4 months instead of 12.

recommended_tiers.name: Employer Welfare Pack (Discounter B2B)
recommended_tiers.target_persona: HR Sandra — Welfare / Dorm-Operator Bulk Buyer
what_in: 100× worker welfare hampers (rice 5kg + oil 1L + noodles + biscuits + soap)
Multi-site delivery: up to 3 dormitory addresses included
GST e-invoice with PEPPOL-compliant line items
NET-30 payment terms with PayNow / corporate credit card / bank transfer
Optional brand-co-wrapping: company logo sticker on the hamper (+S$1.50/unit)
Compliance note: per-unit cost vs FairPrice Xtra Business RRP shown on quote
what_excluded: Custom hamper SKU substitution (curated batches only at this price; SKU custom = +20% per change)
Same-day delivery (3-day lead minimum for multi-site)
recommended_tiers.psychological_anchor: S$15/worker for a hamper that retails at S$28+ at FairPrice Xtra Business — and Sandra gets the audit-friendly e-invoice she actually needs. EDG-bundleable as part of a worker-welfare transformation project.

grants.name: PSG
grants.eligibility: SG-registered SME, ≥30% local shareholding, ≤S$100M revenue or ≤200 employees, IMDA pre-approved e-commerce / logistics / B2B procurement category. Not directly applicable to consumer FMCG retail; relevant only if Discounter B2B portal is listed under e-commerce/procurement enabler.

grants.name: EDG
grants.eligibility: SG-registered, ≥30% local shareholding, applied via Business Grants Portal before project start, bundled with consultancy + implementation deliverables. Most relevant when an employer (Sandra's company) bundles a worker-welfare transformation project that includes Discounter B2B onboarding.

grants.name: WSG WDG(JR+)
grants.eligibility: Employer-funded job-redesign / worker-wellness projects via WSG / Elitez pre-approved consultancy track. Discounter / FlashCart can position as the welfare-supply-chain leg of a broader job-redesign engagement (canteen-cost reduction → worker take-home uplift narrative).

grants.name: SFEC
grants.eligibility: SG enterprise with ≥S$750 SDL paid, ≥3 SG local employees; bundled with approved training / transformation programme. Not applicable to FMCG resale subscriptions; flag only if welfare-pack engagement is wrapped with HR transformation training.
